answer: The passage where Helena (Elene) interrogates the Jews about the location of the True Cross is found in Cynewulf's Elene, work slug 'cynewulf-elene-d2d132', chapter 1. The interrogation unfolds across two main paragraphs (p-7b1c1f and p-f5d1f1). Helena arrives in Jerusalem and first summons 3,000 Jewish scholars, rebuking them for rejecting Christ and demanding they reveal what she seeks. She then narrows the group to 1,000, then 500. When the 500 still deny knowledge, she threatens to burn them alive ('on a hill a pyre shall consume you, the hottest of death-flames') if they keep concealing the truth. Terrified, they offer up Judas as their wisest man. Helena interrogates Judas alone: she demands he reveal where the Rood (Cross) is buried, offering him the choice of 'life or death.' When he feigns ignorance, she presses him by asking how Jews can remember the Trojan War in detail yet claim to forget the Crucifixion. After he continues to stall, she swears by God that he will be tormented by hunger, then has him cast into a dry well for seven nights. On the seventh day, weakened by hunger, Judas cries out that he will reveal the holy tree. He is freed, goes to the hill of Calvary, prays in Hebrew, and a column of smoke rises; he digs twenty feet down and finds three crosses buried together.
